data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a pandemic of tularemia (rabbit fever) transmitted through the international trade in wool and hides, with Egypt surviving due to quarantine practices linked to mummification ritual purity","mechanism": "Zoonotic pathogen transmission along the Anatolian-Greek-Levantine wool exchange network; the bacterium Francisella tularensis spreads via aerosolized wool dust and tick vectors; palace economies collapsed when herding labor forces and textile workshops experienced 40-60% mortality; Egypt's natron-based corpse-disinfection rituals inadvertently created barrier nursing protocols that limited transmission","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "textile economies", "palace workshop systems"], "excludes": ["Egyptian military resilience", "Sea Peoples as causal", "climate determinism", "volcanic mechanisms"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological or textual evidence of mass graves with tularemia-specific bone lesions in palace-period contexts", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "DNA or protein residue from F. tularensis in contemporaneous wool samples or burial contexts", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian papyri or inscriptions referencing epidemic wool-quarantine or natron-based disease prevention during the 19th-20th Dynasties", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Survival of non-textile-specialized palace economies or regions with minimal wool trade (e.g., Cyprus copper economy)", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Deliberately atypical candidate testing pathogenic determinism against the archaeological silence on disease; exploits Egypt's ritual distinctiveness as functional adaptation; vulnerable to absence of bioarchaeological evidence and anachronistic bacteriology"}
